sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a history of a cough that has been present off and on for approximately three months.
The cough started after a period of heavy physical activity involving loading horses and was initially thought to be a cold.
The patient's primary care physician diagnosed bronchitis and performed a chest x-ray to rule out pneumonia, which revealed a lung nodule.
A CT scan confirmed the nodule and the patient was referred to our office.
The patient is nervous about the diagnosis and is seeking reassurance.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly.
The patient was a previous smoker, quitting approximately seven years ago.
The patient's mother had breast cancer, but is currently doing well.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain or shortness of breath.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/76, heart rate 70, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, afebrile.
Physical exam: The lungs are clear and equal bilaterally, with no evidence of wheezing or crackles.
The heart has a regular rate and rhythm, with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs.
The extremities have intact pulses and capillary refill is brisk, although there is some swelling in the knuckles consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.
Test Results: A CT scan revealed a solitary 2 cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which appears smooth in appearance and has no evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule that requires further evaluation and management.
Problem list: Lung nodule confirmed; Rheumatoid Arthritis; History of smoking; Family history of breast cancer.
Plan:
I plan to order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to assess lung function and a PET CT scan to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
I will also schedule a video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the nodule.
The patient will be under general anesthesia and will have a chest tube inserted post-procedure.
The patient will be admitted to the hospital for one night and discharged the following day.
I will also instruct the patient to continue following up with their rheumatologist and to continue their medication therapy as previously outlined.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in a few minutes to sign paperwork and begin the treatment process.
